intro: AI tools can be incredibly useful, but they have important limitations. This module explores how to use AI outputs critically and responsibly.

scenarios.label: Scenario: Research References
scenarios.text: You're writing a paper on climate change. You ask an AI tool for academic sources and it gives you a list of 10 references with author names and journal titles.

options.text: Use all sources without checking — the AI found them so they must be valid.
Verify each source actually exists and is credible before citing it.
Cite the ones that look the most impressive and ignore the rest.

scenarios.feedbackCorrect: AI tools frequently hallucinate references — they can produce author names, titles, and journals that don't actually exist. Always verify every source in an academic database.
scenarios.feedbackIncorrect: AI tools frequently hallucinate references. They can produce author names, titles, and journals that don't actually exist. You should always verify sources independently.

intro: Protecting your personal information is crucial when using AI tools. What you share can be stored, reviewed, or used in ways you don't expect.

scenarios.label: Scenario: Study Group Help
scenarios.text: You want to use AI to help schedule a study group. You need to include some participant details in your prompt.

options.text: Include everyone's full names, emails and phone numbers for accuracy.
Use generic identifiers like 'Student A, Student B' and avoid real personal details.
Only share first names — that should be safe enough.

scenarios.feedbackCorrect: Using generic identifiers protects your classmates' privacy. You don't need real personal data for AI to help you with scheduling or planning.
scenarios.feedbackIncorrect: Even first names can be personal data. Use generic placeholders when possible. Real names and contact details should never be shared with AI tools.

intro: How you ask questions matters. Ethical prompting means asking for guidance and understanding — not outsourcing your thinking to the AI.

scenarios.label: Scenario: Essay Assignment
scenarios.text: You have an essay due on the causes of World War I. You want to use AI to help.

options.text: "Write me a 1500-word essay on the causes of World War I."
"Help me create an outline for an essay on the main causes of World War I."
"Give me a complete analysis I can use as my essay."

scenarios.feedbackCorrect: Asking for an outline keeps you in control of the thinking and writing. You develop understanding — AI helps you structure it.
scenarios.feedbackIncorrect: Asking AI to write the entire essay or give you a complete analysis bypasses the learning process and likely violates your institution's academic integrity policies.

intro: Understanding the line between ethical AI assistance and academic dishonesty is essential for your success and credibility.

scenarios.label: Scenario: Take-Home Exam
scenarios.text: You have a take-home exam where the instructions say 'work must be your own.'

options.text: Use AI to explain concepts you don't understand, then write your answers in your own words.
Have AI generate answers, then slightly rephrase them before submitting.
Submit AI-generated answers — it's take-home, so it must be allowed.

scenarios.feedbackCorrect: Using AI to understand concepts, then writing your own answers, is ethical. You're using AI as a teacher — the thinking and expression are still yours.
scenarios.feedbackIncorrect: 'Work must be your own' means your thinking and writing — not AI's. Rephrasing AI output is still academic dishonesty. Always ask your instructor if you're uncertain.
